I separated the patient population into subgroups which initially were only of Male and Female since it is obvious that sex has a significant role in the development of cardiac disease. The goal was to determine if men and women perform predictably equally and, if not, to determine why. Male and female test sets were created from the test data. Table 4 illustrates a confusion Matrix from the caret package which was used to evaluate sex by converting predictions for these subgroups into factor variables. To make the process more effective, it was incorporated into a function. Following the receipt of predictions, the test dataset, and the subgroup identification, this procedure provides a confusion matrix for the specified subgroup. It also determines the Area Under the Curve and generates a ROC curve for additional confirmation.

\newpage

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,echo=FALSE}



# Male matrix and metrics
male_matrix <- matrix(c(47, 5, 8, 90), 2, byrow = TRUE)
male_accuracy <- sum(diag(male_matrix)) / sum(male_matrix)
male_sensitivity <- male_matrix[1,1] / sum(male_matrix[1,])
male_specificity <- male_matrix[2,2] / sum(male_matrix[2,])

# Female matrix and metrics
female_matrix <- matrix(c(26, 2, 1, 4), 2, byrow = TRUE)
female_accuracy <- sum(diag(female_matrix)) / sum(female_matrix)
female_sensitivity <- female_matrix[1,1] / sum(female_matrix[1,])
female_specificity <- female_matrix[2,2] / sum(female_matrix[2,])

# Convert matrices to data frames
male_stats <- as.data.frame(male_matrix)
female_stats <- as.data.frame(female_matrix)

# Rename columns
colnames(male_stats) <- c("Reference 0", "Reference 1")
colnames(female_stats) <- c("Reference 0", "Reference 1")

# Add Prediction column
male_stats$Prediction <- c(0, 1)
female_stats$Prediction <- c(0, 1)

# Add accuracy, sensitivity and specificity as new rows and round them
male_stats <- rbind(male_stats, c(round(male_accuracy, 2), "", "Accuracy"), 
                   c(round(male_sensitivity, 2), "", "Sensitivity"), 
                   c(round(male_specificity, 2), "", "Specificity"))

female_stats <- rbind(female_stats, c(round(female_accuracy, 2), "", "Accuracy"), 
                      c(round(female_sensitivity, 2), "", "Sensitivity"), 
                      c(round(female_specificity, 2), "", "Specificity"))

# Combine both data frames side by side
combined_stats <- cbind(male_stats, female_stats[, 1:2])
colnames(combined_stats) <- c("Male Reference 0", "Male Reference 1", "Metrics", "Female Reference 0", "Female Reference 1")

# Use kable to render the table
kable(combined_stats, align = c('c', 'c', 'l', 'c', 'c'), booktabs = TRUE, row.names = FALSE, caption = "Confusion Matrix: Males vs Females") %>%
  kable_styling()


```

It also determines the Area Under the Curve and generates a ROC curve for additional confirmation.

In this investigation, the model performed a little bit better for males (AUC = 0.90) than for women (AUC = 0.81). The distribution of other predictor variables or differences in the prevalence of heart disease based on gender may be the cause of this discrepancy. The dataset has a higher proportion of males (725) than women (193) and men have a higher frequency of heart disease (458) than women (50), which demonstrates a significant example of class disparity. This imbalance presumably has an effect on how the model performs across subgroups.

Due to unbalanced datasets, machine learning algorithms are unable to learn the characteristics of underrepresented classes. Because the pool was larger for the model to make draws from, it probably caused it to perform better for males. Even though both groups had high AUCs, the lower AUC for females points to a lower level of predictive ability. The confusion matrix for both groups shows high accuracy as shown in (Table 4), but due to class imbalances, accuracy isn't the best performance indicator in this situation. Males showed higher model sensitivity, indicating better positive case detection, an important aspect of medical diagnosis.

# PCA and Pre-clustering

Applying the Elbow plot, a within-cluster sum of squares (WSS) metric was utilized to establish the ideal number of clusters for the dataset. The WSS for a k range between 1 and 10 was measured for each iteration of the k-means clustering. For each experiment up to 15 iterations and 50 randomly chosen starting positions were used. On a line graph displaying the data, the number of clusters and the WSS were shown. The optimal cluster number (3 clusters) was determined to be at the "elbow" of the plot, where adding clusters has no effect on WSS. Condensing the clusters while reducing model complexity is the goal of this technology.

As a first step in creating prediction models, K-means was first employed to cluster the dataset. Due to the datas high dimensionality, utilizing it as-is for clustering might provide mediocre groups. PCA, or Principal Component Analysis, was used in the analysis to shed light on the best course of action. By determining the dimensions or "principal components" that contain the most variation, PCA is a dimensionality reduction approach that lessens the complexity of any dataset. After scaling the data, the first two principal components were computed and shown. The k-means clusters were color-coded for clarity.

This method was replicated using the second clustering technique known as hierarchical clustering. Figure 8 shows when the PCA plots for both clustering techniques were visually analysed, the hierarchical clusters showed greater separation with less overlap than the k-means clusters. This discovery revealed that hierarchical clustering would offer more distinct cluster groupings leadeing to prediction models that were more interpretable.

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE}

## Clustering
set.seed(12)
## Kmeans

dummies_model <- dummyVars(HeartDisease ~ ., data=df)
dataset_numeric <- data.frame(predict(dummies_model, newdata = df))
# Perform PCA
pca_result <- prcomp(scaled_data)

k <- kmeans(dataset_numeric, centers=3)  # 3 clusters


df$cluster <- as.factor(k$cluster)



# Split data into train and test sets

splitIndex <- createDataPartition(df$HeartDisease, p=0.8, list=FALSE)
train_set3 <- df[splitIndex, ]
test_set3 <- df[-splitIndex, ]


# Visualizing the data using the first two principal components
pca_data <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x)
pca_data$cluster <- df$cluster  # Add cluster information to the PCA data

# Plotting the first two principal components

pc1=ggplot(pca_data, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color=as.factor(cluster))) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(color="Cluster") +
  ggtitle("K-means clustering")

```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,echo=FALSE}
## Hierchichal

# Assuming the scaled data is stored in a variable called scaled_data
d <- daisy(scaled_data, metric = "gower")
fit <- hclust(d)

# Cut tree into k clusters
k <- 3 # or another number based on the domain knowledge or dendrogram analysis
clusters_hierarchical <- cutree(fit, k)

# Add the new cluster assignments back to the original data
df$cluster_hierarchical <- clusters_hierarchical



# Visualizing the data using the first two principal components
pca_data <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x)
pca_data$clusterh <- df$cluster_hierarchical  


# Plotting the first two principal components

pc2=ggplot(pca_data, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color=as.factor(clusterh))) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(color="Cluster") +
  ggtitle("Hierarchical clustering")





```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,echo=FALSE,fig.cap="First two principle components plotted and seperated by cluster to showcase distinction in cluster assignments"}
# Combine the plots side by side with a main title and two subtitles
grid.arrange(pc1, pc2, ncol = 2, 
             top = textGrob("PCA Plot of First Two Principal Components", gp = gpar(fontsize = 16, fontface = "bold")),
             bottom = textGrob("", gp = gpar(fontsize = 12)))

```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE}

# Summary statistics for hierarchical clusters
aggregate(.~ cluster_hierarchical, data=df, mean) # for new cluster summaries

# Assuming the finalized cluster column is named 'cluster_hierarchical'
trainIndex4 <- sample(1:nrow(df), 0.8 * nrow(df))
train_set4 <- df[trainIndex4, ]
test_set4 <- df[-trainIndex4, ]


# Convert cluster_hierarchical to factor
train_set4$cluster_hierarchical <- as.factor(train_set4$cluster_hierarchical)
test_set4$cluster_hierarchical <- as.factor(test_set4$cluster_hierarchical)

# Fit the Random Forest model on the training data
rf_model_kmeans <- randomForest(cluster ~ ., data=train_set3, ntree=100)

# Make predictions on the test data
predictions_kmeans <- predict(rf_model_kmeans, newdata=test_set3)

# Build Random Forest Model
rf_model <- randomForest(cluster_hierarchical ~ ., data=train_set4, ntree=100)

# Predict on test data
predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata=test_set4)
```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE}

confusionMatrix(predictions,test_set4$cluster_hierarchical)
# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(predictions_kmeans, test_set3$cluster)
```

As evidence, cluster assignments were predicted using random forest models for both approaches. Random Forest was selected due to its advantages against overfitting and capacity for large dimensional datasets. The performance of the models was evaluated by constructing the confusion matrix for the expected and actual cluster assignments. The k-means model outperformed the hierarchical model by a small margin. This result contradicted the concept of our first PCA plot (Figure 8). This demonstrates the need of linking quantitative measures with interpretations of qualitative data. Because k-means uses Euclidean distance, which is not ideal for categorical variables, there is only a slight variation in performance (Accuracy). The initial conclusion of using hierarchical clustering remains.

The results indicate how useful PCA is for emphasizing the underlying structure of the data and guiding the selection of the most appropriate clustering method. This procedure also showed how combining clustering techniques with dimensionality reduction techniques and predictive modelling may be advantageous for predictive analytics projects. Future research may benefit from examining the use of additional dimensionality reduction techniques when used with clustering algorithms to increase the effectiveness of predictive modelling.

# Clustering

It is typical practise when using predictive modelling to assess model performance across the board and within particular clusters of the data. Particularly for datasets that are diverse in a number of dimensions, this issue is significant. By breaking down assessment into different clusters, it is simpler to comprehend how the model performs on particular subgroups and enhance the model's performance. In this study, I use a prediction model created to evaluate the risk of heart disease as an example of one such strategy.

I had created a prediction model using many patient variables to forecast instances of heart disease. Despite the fact that the model performed well on the entire test set of data, I used the data to examine the model's performance across different hierarchical clusters. After preliminary assessments, hierarchical clustering was used to separate the data into clusters. The clustering process integrated the Gower metric to efficiently handle both categorical and numerical variables. The elbow plot and dendrogram analysis results led to the division of the dendrogram tree into three clusters (k=3). Those clusters were then assigned back to the original data, and named as a new variable.

Understanding the model's performance on each cluster independently was the main objective. A logistic regression model, a random forest model, and a support vector machine (SVM) model were used to fit the training data for each cluster, and predictions were generated for each cluster. For logistic regression and SVM, metrics like accuracy and the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) were computed. While accuracy provided a more straightforward understanding of the model complexity than AIC, a measure of the relative quality of statistical models, accuracy provided insight into how well the model predicted instances of heart disease.

The out-of-bag (OOB) error for the random forest model, a type of cross-validation approach specific to the random forest model, gave an objective assessment of the model error in addition to accuracy.


## **Cluster attributes**

Cluster 1: Younger, Healthy Individuals

-   The Average age is approximately 47, which is the youngest among the three clusters.

-   Patients have a Chest Pain Type score of about 2.12, which is the highest among the clusters, indicating they experience less severe types of chest pain.

-   Resting Blood Pressure and Cholesterol levels are relatively lower, suggesting better cardiovascular health.

-   Their Max Heart Rate during exercise is the highest among the clusters, again indicating better cardiovascular health.

-   This cluster has the lowest Oldpeak value, which means they have less exercise-induced ST depression, another indicator of good heart health.

-   This group has the lowest Heart Disease score (1.23), suggesting a lower prevalence of heart disease compared to the other clusters.

Cluster 2: Older Individuals with Medium Risk

-   Average age is approximately 57, which is higher than Cluster 1.

-   This group experiences more severe types of chest pain (Chest Pain Type score: 1.65).

-   They have a higher Resting Blood Pressure and slightly lower Cholesterol level compared to Cluster 1.

-   Their Max Heart Rate during exercise is significantly lower than Cluster 1, indicating potential cardiovascular issues.

-   This cluster has a higher Oldpeak value, indicating more exercise-induced ST depression and thus potentially higher risk of heart issues.

-   The Heart Disease score for this group is 1.65, which is higher than Cluster 1, suggesting a medium level of heart disease prevalence.

Cluster 3: High Risk Individuals

-   Average age is around 56, similar to Cluster 2.

-   This group experiences the most severe types of chest pain (Chest Pain Type score: 1.52).

-   They have comparable Resting Blood Pressure to Cluster 1 but with much lower Cholesterol level, which may due to medical treatment for diagnosed heart conditions.

-   Their Max Heart Rate during exercise is the lowest among the clusters, indicating serious cardiovascular health issues.

-   This cluster has the highest Oldpeak value, indicating the most significant exercise-induced ST depression and therefore the highest risk of heart disease.

-   The Heart Disease score for this group is highest at 1.88, suggesting a high prevalence of heart disease.


```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,echo=FALSE,fig.cap="Iterative loops testing three model types on three clusters and assessing the performance with accuracy and margin of error."}
# Set number of iterations
n_iter <- 100

# Create an empty data frame to store results
results <- data.frame(Iteration = integer(), Cluster = integer(), Model = character(), Accuracy = numeric())

# Perform n_iter iterations
for (iter in 1:n_iter) {
  
  # Split data into train and test sets
  set.seed(iter) # change the seed at each iteration to get different splits
  splitIndex <- createDataPartition(df$HeartDisease, p=0.8, list=FALSE)
  train_set3 <- df[splitIndex, ]
  test_set3 <- df[-splitIndex, ]
  
  # Fit and assess model for each cluster
  for (i in sort(unique(df$cluster))) {
    
    train_set_cluster <- train_set3[train_set3$cluster == i, ]
    test_set_cluster <- test_set3[test_set3$cluster == i, ]
    
    # Exclude 'cluster' variable
    train_set_cluster$cluster <- NULL
    test_set_cluster$cluster <- NULL
    
    # Fit the model
    log_model <- glm(HeartDisease ~ ., family="binomial", data=train_set_cluster)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions <- predict(log_model, newdata=test_set_cluster, type="response")
    
    # Convert predictions to binary outcome
    predictions_bin <- ifelse(predictions > 0.5, 1, 0)
    
    # Compute accuracy
    accuracy <- sum(predictions_bin == test_set_cluster$HeartDisease) / nrow(test_set_cluster)
    
    # Add results to the data frame
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(Iteration = iter, Cluster = i, Model = "Logistic Regression", Accuracy = accuracy))
    
    # Fit the Random Forest model
    rf_model <- randomForest(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set_cluster, importance=TRUE)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions_rf <- predict(rf_model, newdata=test_set_cluster)
    
    # Compute accuracy
    accuracy_rf <- sum(predictions_rf == test_set_cluster$HeartDisease) / nrow(test_set_cluster)
    
    # Add results to the data frame
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(Iteration = iter, Cluster = i, Model = "Random Forest", Accuracy = accuracy_rf))
    
    # Fit the SVM model
    svm_model <- svm(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set_cluster, kernel = "radial", cost = 10, scale = FALSE)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions_svm <- predict(svm_model, newdata=test_set_cluster)
    
    # Compute accuracy
    accuracy_svm <- sum(predictions_svm == test_set_cluster$HeartDisease) / nrow(test_set_cluster)
    
    # Add results to the data frame
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(Iteration = iter, Cluster = i, Model = "SVM", Accuracy = accuracy_svm))
  }
}

# Create a mapping for cluster names
cluster_names <- c(
  "1" = "Young-Healthy ",
  "2" = "Old-Medium Risk",
  "3" = "High-Risk"
)

# Create a color mapping for each model 
model_colors <- c(
  "Logistic Regression" = "darkred",
  "Random Forest" = "darkgreen",
  "SVM" = "gold"
)

# Calculate average accuracy and standard error for each model and cluster
avg_results <- results %>%
  group_by(Model, Cluster) %>%
  summarise(Avg_Accuracy = mean(Accuracy),
            SE_Accuracy = sd(Accuracy) / sqrt(n()),
            .groups='keep') %>%
  mutate(Cluster_Name = factor(cluster_names[as.character(Cluster)], levels = cluster_names))

# Plot the average accuracy with error bars
ggplot(avg_results, aes(x=Cluster_Name, y=Avg_Accuracy, fill=Model)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", position=position_dodge(), aes(fill = Model)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_colors) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=Avg_Accuracy-SE_Accuracy, ymax=Avg_Accuracy+SE_Accuracy), 
                position=position_dodge(0.9), width=0.2) +
  labs(x="Cluster", y="Average Accuracy", title="Average Model Performance by Cluster with Error Bars") +
  theme_minimal()

```

Figure 9 shows the findings, it was discovered that the accuracy and AIC values for the logistic regression model differed amongst the clusters. This variant proves that the model handled various clusters with varied degrees of success. The same results were also apparent for the random forest and SVM models with varying degrees of fluctuating between clusters.

For instance, clusters 2 and 3 had the highest accuracy of the logistic regression model and the lowest accuracy for cluster 1. Similar results were shown with the random forest model, which had the best accuracy on clusters 2 and 3. SVM, however performed best for cluster 3 and 1. Since clusters differ inherently in terms of both the target variable distribution and the predictor factors, this variation in performance measures may be sensible.

In the aim of ensuring robustness and efficacy, this paper highlighted the significance of evaluating predictive model performance across several clusters. By doing this, possible areas for improvement can be identified which will lead to a better understanding of how a model will perform on various demographic groups. To prevent over generalization and boost predictive prowess, the developers of predictive models ought to include such cluster-based assessments as a standard component of model evaluation expemplified by (Figure 9).



```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE, results='asis'}
# Convert avg_results to a matrix
avg_results_matrix <- data.matrix(avg_results[, -c(1, 2)])  # exclude the factor columns
rownames(avg_results_matrix) <- paste(avg_results$Model, avg_results$Cluster, sep = "_")  # create unique row names

# Use stargazer function
stargazer(avg_results_matrix,
          type = 'latex',
          header = F,
          title = "Average Results Summary",
          rownames = TRUE,
          digits=2)





```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE}
##

# Compute and plot wss for k = 2 to k = 10
k.max <- 10
wss <- sapply(1:k.max, 
              function(k){kmeans(df_encoded, k, nstart=50, iter.max = 15 )$tot.withinss})

plot(1:k.max, wss,
     type="b", pch = 19, frame = FALSE, 
     xlab="Number of clusters K",
     ylab="Total within-clusters sum of squares")

```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE}
# Attempt to sort cluster by attributes

# Calculate the mean of each numeric attribute for each cluster
numeric_vars <- sapply(df, is.numeric)
mean_by_cluster <- aggregate(df[numeric_vars], by=list(df$cluster), mean)

# Calculate the mode of each categorical attribute for each cluster
categorical_vars <- sapply(df, is.factor)
mode_by_cluster <- lapply(df[categorical_vars], function(x) tapply(x, df$cluster, function(x) names(which.max(table(x)))))

# Combine both data frames into one for easy viewing
attributes_by_cluster <- cbind(mean_by_cluster, mode_by_cluster)


# Cluster summaries

# Summary statistics for Cluster 1
print("Summary Statistics for Cluster 1")
summary(df[df$cluster == 1,])

# Summary statistics for Cluster 2
print("Summary Statistics for Cluster 2")
summary(df[df$cluster == 2,])

# Summary statistics for Cluster 3
print("Summary Statistics for Cluster 3")
summary(df[df$cluster == 3,])

```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,include=FALSE}
## lasso

full_model <- glm(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set2, family = binomial)
step_model <- stepAIC(full_model, direction = "backward")
summary(step_model)

# Make predictions on the validation set
step_model_preds_prob <- predict(step_model, newdata = test_set2, type = "response")
step_model_preds <- ifelse(step_model_preds_prob > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Compute performance metrics
step_model_accuracy <- sum(step_model_preds ==
                             test_set2$HeartDisease_1) / nrow(test_set2)
step_model_auc <- pROC::auc(pROC::roc(test_set2$HeartDisease_1, 
                                      step_model_preds_prob))





x_train <- model.matrix(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set2)[, -1]
y_train <- train_set2$HeartDisease_1

x_val <- model.matrix(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = test_set2)[, -1]
y_val <- test_set2$HeartDisease_1

lasso_model <- glmnet(x_train, y_train, family = "binomial", alpha = 1)
cv.lasso <- cv.glmnet(x_train, y_train, family = "binomial", alpha = 1)
best_lambda <- cv.lasso$lambda.min

# Make predictions on the validation set
lasso_model_preds_prob <- predict(lasso_model, newx = x_val, 
                                  s = best_lambda, type = "response")
lasso_model_preds <- ifelse(lasso_model_preds_prob > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Compute performance metrics
lasso_model_accuracy <- 
  sum(lasso_model_preds == 
        test_set2$HeartDisease_1) / nrow(test_set2)
lasso_model_auc <- pROC::auc(pROC::roc(test_set2$HeartDisease_1, 
                                       lasso_model_preds_prob))


# Print out the metrics for comparison
cat("Stepwise selection logistic regression - Accuracy: ", 
    step_model_accuracy, " AUC: ", step_model_auc, "\n")
cat("Lasso logistic regression - Accuracy: ", lasso_model_accuracy, 
    " AUC: ", lasso_model_auc, "\n")

```

\newpage

# **Communicating data with interactive app**

**Shiny app link : https://hqapp2.shinyapps.io/Heartapp2/**



This paper seeks to make the findings of the study accessible to both specialists and non-experts in a straightforward and efficient manner. Shiny, an interactive web application available in R, was utilized to accomplish this goal. It is a powerful tool for building interactive apps right in the R environment. This serves as foundation of the data communication aspect of this research. The app presents the visual output of most important conclusions made from the study in an effort to effectively convey the research findings. This exemplifies how different methods and ideas used throughout the study to predict heart disease interact with one another.


![The app interface.](/Users/hassanainqazzaz/Downloads/C5.png)

A user interface (UI) is required as the base to develop an interactive programme, shown in (Figure 10). In order for any user to browse with ease, it was crucial to make sure the user interface (UI) was easy to use and intuitive. Users can enter vital patient data in the input fields on the sidebar, which include the patient's age, sex, type of chest pain, resting blood pressure, cholesterol level, fasting blood sugar, resting ECG, maximum heart rate, exercise-induced angina, old peak, and ST slope. The UI consists of two main components: the left sidebar and the main window.

\break

![Interactive predicted risk of heart disease](/Users/hassanainqazzaz/Downloads/C1.png)

In the primary section of the interface, which also acts as the output display, the forecast result of the model is shown through visual representation and numerical outputs. The display includes two complementary plots, the patient's predicted cluster and risk of developing heart disease, as well as the predicted cluster's distribution of risks by cluster and the relationship between age and risk of developing heart disease shown in (Figure 11). While the second plot is interactive and input-based, the first plot is fixed. The "Cerulean" motif is applied to create a straightforward yet eye-catching design.

![Interactive predicted cluster assignment and respective cluster description](/Users/hassanainqazzaz/Downloads/C2.png)

The app's strength is shown in the server function. In order to generate forecasts and create visualizations, it is where all algorithms and computations are merged. Reactive values are created in relation to the patient's data, and these values then create a new data frame in reaction to user input. This responsiveness ensures that the forecasts and visualizations are dynamically updated when users change their input. The dataset's risk was assessed using the random forest model, which also produced a density map of the expected risk for each cluster in the main panel. By providing a detailed overview of the distribution of risk across multiple patient subgroups, this map provides a comparative context for individual patient risk projections.

After that, a prediction is calculated to determine which cluster a new patient will belong to. Intricate hierarchical clustering and selecting the cluster based on the least average connectivity were done using the AGNES technique. The predicted cluster is then displayed in the main panel along with its name and a brief explanation, making it simple for non-experts to comprehend the result shown in (Figure 12). Additionally, the new patient's likelihood of acquiring heart disease is predicted using the random forest model. The expected risk is represented by a percentage in this.

However, to give the user a more comprehensive understanding of the patient's risk, a supplemental section of the main panel includes a scatter plot of age vs. estimated risk of heart disease, shown in (Figure 13). This plot, which provides a visual representation of the patient's risk in proportion to the entire dataset, highlights the age and danger of the new patient. The new patient is represented as a red point on the plot that shifts vertically and horizontally depending on risk and age. In addition, a circle surrounds the point, signifying the prediction's error margin, which can be changed depending on the input.

\newpage

![Interactive plot showing new patient's risk and margin of error](/Users/hassanainqazzaz/Downloads/C3.png)

The app's goal was to transfer all technical analyses into a dynamic, interactive channel of communication that non-technical people could use to interact with it and get real-time forecasts for probable new circumstances. By using complex visualizations like the density and scatter plots, improved understanding of these predictions was achieved by providing a clear, comparative context. This study provides a concrete illustration of how interactive data visualization and predictive modelling may be used to create trustworthy, beneficial applications in the healthcare industry. Similar methodologies may result in greatly enhanced diagnostic procedures, better individualized patient care, and generally enhanced healthcare services.



# **Limitations**

The study was fruitful with results, some expected some not, however it wasn't free of limitations and roadblocks. Primarily, the source of data was vague with no information available on the collection methods, hospitals, procedures nor purpose of studies conducted, which made all measurements and variables be taken with a grain of salt. Secondary, due to the length constraints of the thesis, much analysis remains to be done and new concepts to be explored which leaves this work as good foundation to build upon. Tertiary, the shiny app domain being a free service mandated a limit on the CPU and memory usage the app can use. As such, the app had to be dramatically trimmed down which left many plots, numerical outputs, and visual adjustments to be left out.


# **Conclusion**


This paper introduced a wide range of advanced statistical methods and approaches, set out to a comprehensive exploration of the intricate relationships of heart disease predictors. Furthermore, the analysis methodically constructed a solid dataset, ready for rigorous analysis, by first utilizing data pre-processing techniques including clustering and Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Under multiple pre-processing approaches (One-hot encoding and scaling) and multiple assessment criteria (ROC, AIC,Residuals) three main algorithms were tested. To a surprising finding, the logistic regression performed equally or even better than the black-box methods used for comparison (Random forest & SVM). This finding serves both interpretability and resource allocation as the logistic model is the simplest of the three and easiest to interpret. Initially a logistic model with all the variables was fitted to percieve the relationship of the variables to the outcome, however for the final in-app prediction a tuned stepwise model was used. 

The primary analytical instrument, the logistic regression algorithm provided interesting findings, from the distinct impact of gender on heart disease risk to the subtleties of various types of chest discomfort and its implications. The model performed well according to diagnostic tests with the residuals showing some skewness,suggesting room for improvement. By addressing these slight differences, we may be able to improve our model's predictive prowess and increase its value as a tool for medical professionals.

Additionally, by using methods to alter p-values, the model's inferences were made to be both statistically and clinically useful. These rigorous validation procedures highlight the research's dedication to empirical rigor. The Shiny app, designed as the study's application, summaries the research's key findings in a way that makes them simple to access and understand for healthcare practitioners.

 

To conclude, this analysis made major strides in understanding and predicting the drivers of heart disease, however, it also emphasizes the value of further research. The quest to completely understand cardiac disease is continuing due to the meshing of biology, environment, and possibly still unidentified variables. Nevertheless,  the groundwork established in this thesis could be used as foundation to future initiatives which keep advancing in the direction of more precise, implementable, and patient-centered solutions in the fight against heart disease.






\newpage

# **Bibliography**


Ahmad, T., Lund, L. H., Rao, P., et al. (2018). Machine Learning Methods Improve Prognostication, Identify Clinically Distinct Phenotypes, and Detect Heterogeneity in Response to Therapy in a Large Cohort of Heart Failure Patients. Journal of the American Heart Association, 7(8), e008081.

Benjamin E.J., Muntner P., Alonso A. et al. (2019). Heart Disease and Stroke Statistics-2019 Update: A Report From the American Heart Association. Circulation, 139(10):e56-e528.

Bleumink, G. S., Knetsch, A. M., Sturkenboom, M. C. et al. (2004). Quantifying the heart failure epidemic: prevalence, incidence rate, lifetime risk and prognosis of heart failure The Rotterdam Study. European Heart Journal, 25(18), 1614–1619.

Borlaug, B. A. (2014). The pathophysiology of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Nature reviews. Cardiology, 11(9), 507–515.

Cannon, B. (2013). Cardiovascular disease: Biochemistry to behaviour. Nature, 493, S2–S3. https://doi.org/10.1038/493S2a

Cleveland Clinic. (2020). Types of Heart Disease. Cleveland Clinic.

Drazner, M. H. (2011). The progression of hypertensive heart disease. Circulation, 123(3), 327-334.

Dunlay, S. M., Roger, V. L., & Redfield, M. M. (2017). Epidemiology of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Nature Reviews Cardiology, 14(10), 591-602.

Eriksson, H., Svärdsudd, K., Larsson, B., et al. (2005). Risk factors for heart failure in the general population: The study of men born in 1913. European Heart Journal, 6(7), 647-656.

Falk, E. (2011). Pathogenesis of atherosclerosis. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 47(8 Suppl), C7-12.

Fihn, S. D., Gardin, J. M., Abrams, J., et al. (2012). 2012 ACCF/AHA/ACP/AATS/PCNA/SCAI/STS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Patients With Stable Ischemic Heart Disease. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 60(24), e44-e164.

Hunt, S. A., Abraham, W. T., Chin, M. H., et al. (2009). 2009 focused update incorporated into the ACC/AHA 2005 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Heart Failure in Adults. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 53(15), e1–e90.

Inglis, S. C., Clark, R. A., McAlister, F. A., et al. (2010). Structured telephone support or telemonitoring programmes for patients with chronic heart failure. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2010(8).

Januzzi, J. L., Camargo, C. A., Anwaruddin, S., et al. (2006). The N-terminal Pro-BNP Investigation of Dyspnea in the Emergency department (PRIDE) study. The American Journal of Cardiology, 95(8), 948-954.

Knuuti, J., Wijns, W., Saraste, A., et al. (2020). 2019 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of chronic coronary syndromes. European Heart Journal, 41(3), 407-477.

Lauer, M. S., Francis, G. S., Okin, P. M., Pashkow, F. J., Snader, C. E., & Marwick, T. H. (1999). Impaired heart rate response to graded exercise: prognostic implications of chronotropic incompetence in the Framingham Heart Study. Circulation, 99(8), 1029-1036.

Levy, D., Kenchaiah, S., Larson, M. G., et al. (2002). Long-term trends in the incidence of and survival with heart failure. New England Journal of Medicine, 347(18), 1397-1402.

Mensah, G. A., & Brown, D. W. (2007). An overview of cardiovascular disease burden in the United States. Health affairs, 26(1), 38-48.

Nishimura, R. A., Otto, C. M., Bonow, R. O., et al. (2017). 2017 AHA/ACC Focused Update of the 2014 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 70(2), 252-289.

Owan, T. E., & Redfield, M. M. (2005). Epidemiology of diastolic heart failure. Progress in cardiovascular diseases, 47(5), 320-332.

Ponikowski, P., Voors, A. A., Anker, S. D., et al. (2016). 2016 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of acute and chronic heart failure. European Heart Journal, 37(27), 2129-2200.

Rajpurkar, P., Irvin, J., Ball, R. L., Zhu, K., Yang, B., Mehta, H., ... & Langlotz, C. P. (2017). Deep learning for chest radiograph diagnosis: A retrospective comparison of the CheXNeXt algorithm to practicing radiologists. PLoS medicine, 15(11), e1002686.

Reddy, Y. N., & Borlaug, B. A. (2016). Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction. Current problems in cardiology, 41(4), 145-88.

Roger, V. L. (2013). Epidemiology of Heart Failure. Circulation Research, 113(6), 646–659.

Savarese, G., & Lund, L. H. (2017). Global Public Health Burden of Heart Failure. Cardiac failure review, 3(1), 7–11.

Steinhubl, S. R., Muse, E. D., & Topol, E. J. (2015). Can mobile health technologies transform health care? JAMA, 310(22), 2395-2396.

Wijeysundera, H. C., Norris, C., Van Walraven, C., Bennell, M. C., Yang, H., Krahn, M., ... & Ko, D. T. (2010). Relationship between initial health care provider and referral for coronary angiography in patients investigated for chest pain. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 55(25), 2861-2868.

Whelton, P. K., Carey, R. M., Aronow, W. S., et al. (2018). 2017 ACC/AHA/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/AGS/APhA/ASH/ASPC/NMA/PCNA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 71(19), e127-e248.

World Health Organization. (2021). Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs). WHO.

Yancy, C. W., Jessup, M., Bozkurt, B., et al. (2013). 2013 ACCF/AHA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure: A Report of the American College of Cardiology Foundation/American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 62(16), e147-e239.




\newpage



# **Appendix**


## **Prepartion & EDA**

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}
library(tidyverse)
library(GGally)
library(dplyr)
library(caret)
library(e1071)
library(randomForest)
library(gbm)
library(pROC)
library(gridExtra)
library(heatmaply)
library(fastDummies)
library(ggfortify)
library(shiny)
library(clustMixType)
library(cluster)
library(plotly)
library(ggalt)
library(ggforce)
library(shinythemes)

# set directory
setwd("/Users/hq/Downloads")


# Read the data
df = read.csv("heart.csv")



# Pre-process

df$Sex <- as.factor(df$Sex)
df$ChestPainType <- as.factor(df$ChestPainType)
df$RestingECG <- as.factor(df$RestingECG)
df$ExerciseAngina <- as.factor(df$ExerciseAngina)
df$ST_Slope <- as.factor(df$ST_Slope)
df$FastingBS = as.factor(df$FastingBS)
df$HeartDisease = as.factor(df$HeartDisease)


# Select only numeric columns
numeric_columns <- sapply(df, is.numeric)
scaled_data <- scale(df[, numeric_columns])


categorical_vars <- c("Sex", "ChestPainType", "FastingBS", "RestingECG", "ExerciseAngina", "ST_Slope")

summaries <- lapply(categorical_vars, function(var) {
  df %>%
    group_by(HeartDisease, !!sym(var)) %>%
    summarise(Count = n())
})

# View summaries
for (i in 1:length(summaries)) {
  print(summaries[[i]])
}


# Summaries

summary(df)

correlation_matrix <- cor(df %>% select_if(is.numeric))
print(correlation_matrix)




# Visualize the data


ggplot(df, aes(x=HeartDisease, y=Cholesterol, fill=HeartDisease)) + 
  geom_boxplot() + theme_minimal() + 
  labs(title="Cholesterol level by Heart Disease status", 
       x="Heart Disease status", y="Cholesterol level")

ggplot(df, aes(x=ChestPainType, fill=HeartDisease)) + 
  geom_bar(position="dodge") + theme_minimal() + 
  labs(title="Heart Disease by Chest Pain Type", x="Chest Pain Type", y="Count")



# RestingBP by heart disease

ggplot(df, aes(x=HeartDisease, y=RestingBP, fill=HeartDisease)) + 
  geom_boxplot() + theme_minimal() + 
  labs(title="Resting Blood Pressure by Heart Disease status", 
       x="Heart Disease status", y="Resting blood pressure")



###



# histogram for age distribution
p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=Age)) +
  geom_histogram(fill='red', color='black', alpha=0.7) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="Age Distribution", x="Age", y="Count")

# bar plot for heart disease cases by sex
p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=Sex, fill=HeartDisease)) +
  geom_bar(position='dodge') +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="Heart Disease Cases by Sex", x="Sex", y="Count")

# box plot for cholesterol level across different chest pain types
p3 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=ChestPainType, y=Cholesterol, fill=ChestPainType)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="Cholesterol Level across Chest Pain Types", x="Chest Pain Type", y="Cholesterol Level")


# scatter plot for Age vs MaxHR
p4 <- ggplot(df, aes(x=Age, y=MaxHR, color=HeartDisease)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.6) + geom_smooth(se=F)+
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="Max Heart Rate vs Age", x="Age", y="Max Heart Rate")

# grid
grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol=2)

grid.arrange(p2, p4)

# interactive heatmap:

heatmaply(correlation_matrix)





# summary table
summary_table <- df %>%
  group_by(HeartDisease) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_Age = mean(Age),
    Mean_RestingBP = mean(RestingBP),
    Mean_Cholesterol = mean(Cholesterol),
    Mean_MaxHR = mean(MaxHR),
    Mean_Oldpeak = mean(Oldpeak)
  )


print(summary_table) ## Make a stargazer table for this



###



# bar plot for each categorical feature
plot_Sex <- df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Sex, fill = as.factor(HeartDisease))) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "Sex", y = "Count") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "HeartDisease", labels = c("No", "Yes"))

plot_ChestPainType <- df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = ChestPainType, fill = as.factor(HeartDisease))) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "Chest Pain Type", y = "Count") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "HeartDisease", labels = c("No", "Yes"))

plot_FastingBS <- df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = FastingBS, fill = as.factor(HeartDisease))) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "Fasting Blood Sugar", y = "Count") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "HeartDisease", labels = c("No", "Yes"))

plot_RestingECG <- df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RestingECG, fill = as.factor(HeartDisease))) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "Resting ECG", y = "Count") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "HeartDisease", labels = c("No", "Yes"))

plot_ExerciseAngina <- df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = ExerciseAngina, fill = as.factor(HeartDisease))) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "Exercise Induced Angina", y = "Count") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "HeartDisease", labels = c("No", "Yes"))

plot_ST_Slope <- df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = ST_Slope, fill = as.factor(HeartDisease))) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "ST Slope", y = "Count") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "HeartDisease", labels = c("No", "Yes"))

# Combine all plots
grid.arrange(plot_Sex, plot_ChestPainType, plot_FastingBS, plot_RestingECG, plot_ExerciseAngina, plot_ST_Slope, ncol = 2)


```

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=TRUE,echo=TRUE}
# For numerical variables
ggplot(df, aes(x=Age, fill=HeartDisease)) + 
  geom_histogram(position="dodge", bins=30) + 
  theme_minimal() + labs(title="Heart Disease by Age", x="Age", y="Count")
```


## **Modelling, tuning and assessing**


```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}


# Mess around with modelling 

set.seed(123)

# Split the data into  (80%) and (20%) sets
index <- createDataPartition(df$HeartDisease, p=0.8, list=FALSE)
train_set <- df[index, ]
test_set <- df[-index, ]


# logistic regression model
logistic_model <- glm(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set, family=binomial())


# summary of the model
summary(logistic_model)

# Predict on the testing set
predictions <- predict(logistic_model, newdata=test_set, type="response")

# Convert probabilities to binary predictions
predictions <- ifelse(predictions > 0.5, 1, 0)

# confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(as.factor(predictions), as.factor(test_set$HeartDisease))


##



##

# Fit the random forest model
rf_model <- randomForest(HeartDisease ~ ., data = train_set)

# Make predictions on the test data
rf_predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_set)

# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(as.factor(rf_predictions), as.factor(test_set$HeartDisease))


###



# Fit the SVM model
svm_model <- svm(HeartDisease ~ ., data = train_set, kernel = "radial")

# Make predictions on the test data
svm_predictions <- predict(svm_model, newdata = test_set)

# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(as.factor(svm_predictions), as.factor(test_set$HeartDisease))




### HYPERPARAMETER TUNING


# Set up cross-validation
control <- trainControl(method="cv", number=10)

# Tune the model
grid <- expand.grid(.mtry=c(1:10)) 

rf_model <- train(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set, method="rf", 
                  trControl=control, tuneGrid=grid)

# Check the best parameters
print(rf_model$bestTune)

# Make predictions on the test data
rf_predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata=test_set)

# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(as.factor(rf_predictions), as.factor(test_set$HeartDisease))



##

# Set up cross-validation
control <- trainControl(method="cv", number=10)

# Tune the model
grid <- expand.grid(.C=c(0.1, 1, 10), .sigma=c(0.1, 1, 10)) 

svm_model <- train(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set, method="svmRadial", 
                   trControl=control, tuneGrid=grid)

# Check the best parameters
print(svm_model$bestTune)

# Make predictions on the test data
svm_predictions <- predict(svm_model, newdata=test_set)

# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(as.factor(svm_predictions), as.factor(test_set$HeartDisease))





## ROC curves

# Logistic Regression
log_reg_model <- glm(HeartDisease ~ ., family = binomial(), data = train_set)

# Make predictions 
log_reg_predictions <- predict(log_reg_model, newdata = test_set, type="response")


# Create the ROC curve
roc_obj <- roc(test_set$HeartDisease, log_reg_predictions)

# Plot the curve
plot(roc_obj, main="ROC Curve for Logistic Regression")

# AUC
cat("AUC for the Logistic Regression model: ", auc(roc_obj))




# Random Forest
rf_model <- randomForest(HeartDisease ~ ., data = train_set)
rf_predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_set, type="prob")[,2] 

# ROC for Random Forest
roc_obj_rf <- roc(test_set$HeartDisease, rf_predictions)

# Plot the ROC curve
plot(roc_obj_rf, main="ROC Curve for Random Forest")
# Print the AUC
cat("AUC for the Random Forest model: ", auc(roc_obj_rf))


# SVM
svm_model <- svm(HeartDisease ~ ., data = train_set, probability = TRUE)
svm_predictions <- predict(svm_model, newdata = test_set, probability = TRUE)
svm_predictions <- attr(svm_predictions, "probabilities")[,2]

# ROC for SVM
roc_obj_svm <- roc(test_set$HeartDisease, svm_predictions)

# Plot the  curve
plot(roc_obj_svm, main="ROC Curve for SVM")
# AUC
cat("AUC for the SVM model: ", auc(roc_obj_svm))



## Feature importance for random forests

# random forest model
rf_model <- randomForest(HeartDisease ~ ., data = train_set)

# Calculate feature importance
importance <- importance(rf_model)

# Create a data frame for plotting
importance_df <- data.frame(
  Feature = row.names(importance),
  Importance = importance[, "MeanDecreaseGini"]  # Use Gini importance
)

importance_df
# Sort by importance
importance_df <- importance_df[order(-importance_df$Importance), ]

# Plot feature importance
ggplot(importance_df, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Importance), y = Importance)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "Feature", y = "Importance (Gini)", title = "Feature Importance")


```


## **Pre-processing and modelling**

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}

### ONE HOT ENCODING


# Apply one-hot encoding to the dataset. 
df_encoded1 <- dummy_cols(df, remove_first_dummy = TRUE)

# Remove the original categorical columns
df_encoded1 <- df_encoded1[ , !(names(df_encoded1) %in% c("Sex", "ChestPainType", 
      "FastingBS", "RestingECG", "ExerciseAngina", "ST_Slope","HeartDisease"))]


# Convert HeartDisease to factor before splitting into training and testing sets
df_encoded1$HeartDisease_1 <- as.factor(df_encoded1$HeartDisease_1)

# split the data and fit the SVM model 
index1 <- createDataPartition(df_encoded1$HeartDisease_1, p=0.8, list=FALSE)
train_set1 <- df_encoded1[index1, ]
test_set1 <- df_encoded1[-index1, ]



# Define training control
train_control <- trainControl(method="cv", number=5)  

# Train the model with Cross-validation
set.seed(123)
svm_model_cv <- train(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data=train_set1, 
                      trControl=train_control, method="svmRadial")

# Summarize the results
print(svm_model_cv)

# Make predictions on the test data
svm_predictions <- predict(svm_model_cv, newdata = test_set1)

# 
confusionMatrix(as.factor(svm_predictions), test_set1$HeartDisease)



###

# logistic regression model
log_model <- glm(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set1, family = "binomial")

# Summarize the model
summary(log_model)

# predictions on the test data
log_probabilities <- predict(log_model, newdata = test_set1, type = "response")
log_predictions <- ifelse(log_probabilities > 0.5, 1, 0)

# confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(as.factor(log_predictions), test_set1$HeartDisease_1)




# random forest model
rf_model1 <- randomForest(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set1)

# predictions on the test data
rf_predictions1 <- predict(rf_model1, newdata = test_set1)

# Evaluate 
confusionMatrix(as.factor(rf_predictions1), as.factor(test_set1$HeartDisease_1))







### ONE HOT ENCODING AND SCALING

# Apply one-hot encoding to the dataset.
df_encoded <- dummy_cols(df, remove_first_dummy = TRUE)

# Remove the original categorical columns
df_encoded <- df_encoded[ , !(names(df_encoded) %in% c("Sex", "ChestPainType", 
                                                       "FastingBS", "RestingECG", "ExerciseAngina", "ST_Slope","HeartDisease"))]


# Convert HeartDisease to factor before scaling
df_encoded$HeartDisease_1 <- as.factor(df_encoded$HeartDisease_1)
df_encoded$Sex_M <- as.factor(df_encoded$Sex_M)
df_encoded$ChestPainType_ATA <- as.factor(df_encoded$ChestPainType_ATA)
df_encoded$ChestPainType_NAP <- as.factor(df_encoded$ChestPainType_NAP)
df_encoded$ChestPainType_TA <- as.factor(df_encoded$ChestPainType_TA)
df_encoded$FastingBS_1 <- as.factor(df_encoded$FastingBS_1)
df_encoded$RestingECG_ST <- as.factor(df_encoded$RestingECG_ST)
df_encoded$RestingECG_Normal <- as.factor(df_encoded$RestingECG_Normal)
df_encoded$ExerciseAngina_Y <- as.factor(df_encoded$ExerciseAngina_Y)
df_encoded$ST_Slope_Up <- as.factor(df_encoded$ST_Slope_Up)
df_encoded$ST_Slope_Flat <- as.factor(df_encoded$ST_Slope_Flat)

# a scaler , fit it on the numerical features of the training data
preProc <- preProcess(df_encoded[, sapply(df_encoded, is.numeric)], method = "scale")

# Use the scaler to transform the numerical features of the entire dataset
df_scaled_encoded <- predict(preProc, newdata = df_encoded)

# split the data and fit models as before
index2 <- createDataPartition(df_scaled_encoded$HeartDisease_1, p=0.8, list=FALSE)
train_set2 <- df_scaled_encoded[index2, ]
test_set2 <- df_scaled_encoded[-index2, ]




# logistic regression model
log_model2 <- glm(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set2, family = "binomial")

# Summarize the model
summary(log_model2)

##

# full model
full_model <- glm(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., family = "binomial", data = train_set2)

# Perform backward selection
reduced_model <- step(full_model, direction = "backward")


##

# predictions on the test data
log_probabilities2 <- predict(reduced_model, newdata = test_set2, type = "response")
log_predictions2 <- ifelse(log_probabilities2 > 0.5, 1, 0)

# confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(as.factor(log_predictions2), test_set2$HeartDisease_1)





# model with Cross-validation
svm_model_cv2 <- train(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data=train_set2, 
                      trControl=train_control, method="svmRadial")

# results
print(svm_model_cv2)

#  predictions on the test data
svm_predictions2 <- predict(svm_model_cv2, newdata = test_set2)

# 
confusionMatrix(as.factor(svm_predictions2), test_set2$HeartDisease_1)





#  random forest model
rf_model2 <- randomForest(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set2)

# predictions on the test data
rf_predictions2 <- predict(rf_model2, newdata = test_set2)

# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(as.factor(rf_predictions2), as.factor(test_set2$HeartDisease_1))






#### TUNING......Necassary



# Define the control via a repeated k-fold cross-validation
fitControl <- trainControl(
  method = "repeatedcv", 
  number = 5, 
  repeats = 3, 
  search = "grid"
)

#  grid for logistic regression

# Lower cost values create a softer margin, higher cost values create a harder margin.
log_grid <- expand.grid(
  .fL = seq(0, 1, length = 10) # Penalty term
)

# Train the model
log_fit <- train(
  HeartDisease_1 ~ ., 
  data = train_set2, 
  method = "glm", 
  family = "binomial",
  trControl = fitControl,
  tuneGrid = log_grid
)

# optimal parameters
print(log_fit$bestTune)

# For SVM, tune the cost and gamma parameters
# Define grid for SVM
svm_grid <- expand.grid(
  .sigma = seq(0.01, 0.1, length = 10),
  .C = c(0.5, 1, 2) 
)

# Train the model
svm_fit <- train(
  HeartDisease_1 ~ ., 
  data = train_set2, 
  method = "svmRadial", 
  trControl = fitControl, 
  tuneGrid = svm_grid
)

# Print the optimal parameters
print(svm_fit$bestTune)



####



### Adjusting P-values



# Get predicted probabilities
predicted_probabilities <- predict(log_model, newdata = test_set1, type = "response")

# Set new threshold
threshold <- 0.55

# Classify as '1' if predicted probability is greater than threshold, else '0'
new_predictions <- ifelse(predicted_probabilities > threshold, 1, 0)

confusionMatrix(as.factor(new_predictions), as.factor(test_set1$HeartDisease_1))


###

# ROC for Random Forest
roc_log_p_new <- roc(test_set1$HeartDisease_1, new_predictions)

# Plot the ROC curve
plot(roc_log_p_new, main="ROC Curve for log model after new p value")
#  AUC
cat("AUC for the log model after new p value: ", auc(roc_log_p_new))




# Random Forest
rf_probabilities <- predict(rf_model1, newdata = test_set1, type = "prob")

# Since predict gives probabilities for both classes (0 and 1), select probability of class '1'
rf_probabilities <- rf_probabilities[,2]

rf_predictions_new <- ifelse(rf_probabilities > 0.55, 1, 0)

confusionMatrix(as.factor(rf_predictions_new), as.factor(test_set1$HeartDisease_1))

# ROC for Random Forest
roc_rf_new <- roc(test_set1$HeartDisease_1, rf_predictions_new)

# Plot the ROC curve
plot(roc_rf_new, main="ROC Curve for RF model after new p value")

# AUC
cat("AUC for the RF model after new p value: ", auc(roc_rf_new))




##

# Train the model with Cross-validation, with probability estimates

svm_model_cv <- train(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data=train_set1, 
                      trControl=train_control, method="svmRadial", 
                      preProcess = c("center", "scale"), # normalization
                      tuneLength = 10, # hyperparameter tuning
                      prob.model = TRUE) # train with probability estimates

# summary
print(svm_model_cv)



# Predict with probability estimates
svm_probabilities <- predict(svm_model_cv, newdata = test_set1, type="prob")

# Extract probabilities for class '1'
svm_probabilities_1 <- svm_probabilities$'1'

# Adjust threshold and create new predictions
svm_predictions_new <- ifelse(svm_probabilities_1 > 0.55, 1, 0)

# Convert to factor and explicitly set the levels
svm_predictions_new <- factor(svm_predictions_new, levels = c('0', '1'))



# confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(as.factor(svm_predictions_new), as.factor(test_set1$HeartDisease_1))


# Convert to numeric
svm_predictions_new_numeric <- as.numeric(as.character(svm_predictions_new))
test_set_numeric <- as.numeric(as.character(test_set1$HeartDisease_1))

# ROC for SVM
roc_svm_new <- roc(test_set_numeric, svm_predictions_new_numeric)

# Plot the ROC curve
plot(roc_svm_new, main="ROC Curve for SVM model after new p value")

# AUC
cat("AUC for the SVM model after new p value: ", auc(roc_svm_new))



```


## **Clustering and subgrouping**

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}

### Comparing model peroformance for different subgroups


# Split the test set into male and female subsets
test_set_male <- test_set[test_set$Sex == "M", ]
test_set_female <- test_set[test_set$Sex == "F", ]

# Get the predicted values for males and females
predictions_male <- predictions[test_set$Sex == "M"]
predictions_female <- predictions[test_set$Sex == "F"]

# Convert both to factors with manually defined levels
predictions_female <- factor(predictions_female, levels = c('0', '1'))
test_set_female$HeartDisease <- factor(test_set_female$HeartDisease, levels = c('0', '1'))

# confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(predictions_female, test_set_female$HeartDisease)


# Convert both to factors with manually defined levels
predictions_male <- factor(predictions_male, levels = c('0', '1'))
test_set_male$HeartDisease <- factor(test_set_male$HeartDisease, levels = c('0', '1'))

# confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(predictions_male, test_set_male$HeartDisease)




###


evaluate_model_for_subgroup <- function(predictions, test_set, subgroup) {
  # Extract subgroup from test_set
  test_set_subgroup <- test_set[test_set$Sex == subgroup, ]
  
  # Get the predicted values for the subgroup
  predictions_subgroup <- predictions[test_set$Sex == subgroup]
  
  # Convert both to factors with manually defined levels
  predictions_subgroup <- factor(predictions_subgroup, levels = c('0', '1'))
  test_set_subgroup$HeartDisease <- factor(test_set_subgroup$HeartDisease, levels = c('0', '1'))
  
  #confusion matrix
  cm <- confusionMatrix(predictions_subgroup, test_set_subgroup$HeartDisease)
  
  # ROC curve
  roc_obj <- roc(as.numeric(as.character(test_set_subgroup$HeartDisease)), 
                 as.numeric(as.character(predictions_subgroup)))
  
  # ROC and AUC
  plot(roc_obj, main=paste("ROC Curve for", subgroup))
  cat("AUC for the", subgroup, "model: ", auc(roc_obj))
  
  return(cm)
}

# Apply function to male and female subgroups
cm_male <- evaluate_model_for_subgroup(predictions, test_set, "M")
cm_female <- evaluate_model_for_subgroup(predictions, test_set, "F")

# confusion matrices
print(cm_male)
print(cm_female)





## Clustering


## Kmeans

dummies_model <- dummyVars(HeartDisease ~ ., data=df)
dataset_numeric <- data.frame(predict(dummies_model, newdata = df))



k <- kmeans(dataset_numeric, centers=3)  # 3 clusters


df$cluster <- as.factor(k$cluster)



# Split data into train and test sets
set.seed(123)
splitIndex <- createDataPartition(df$HeartDisease, p=0.7, list=FALSE)
train_set3 <- df[splitIndex, ]
test_set3 <- df[-splitIndex, ]


## Hierchichal

# scaled data is stored in a variable called scaled_data
d <- daisy(scaled_data, metric = "gower")
fit <- hclust(d)

# Cut tree into k clusters
k <- 3 

clusters_hierarchical <- cutree(fit, k)

# Add the new cluster assignments back to the original data
df$cluster_hierarchical <- clusters_hierarchical


# finalized cluster column is named 'cluster_hierarchical'
trainIndex4 <- sample(1:nrow(df), 0.8 * nrow(df))
train_set4 <- df[trainIndex4, ]
test_set4 <- df[-trainIndex4, ]




# Convert cluster_hierarchical to factor
train_set4$cluster_hierarchical <- as.factor(train_set4$cluster_hierarchical)
test_set4$cluster_hierarchical <- as.factor(test_set4$cluster_hierarchical)

# Set number of iterations
n_iter <- 100

# Create an empty data frame to store results
results <- data.frame(Iteration = integer(), Cluster = integer(), Model = character(), Accuracy = numeric())

# Perform n_iter iterations
for (iter in 1:n_iter) {
  
  # Split data into train and test sets
  set.seed(iter) # change the seed at each iteration to get different splits
  splitIndex <- createDataPartition(df$HeartDisease, p=0.8, list=FALSE)
  train_set3 <- df[splitIndex, ]
  test_set3 <- df[-splitIndex, ]
  
  # Fit and assess model for each cluster
  for (i in sort(unique(df$cluster))) {
    
    train_set_cluster <- train_set3[train_set3$cluster == i, ]
    test_set_cluster <- test_set3[test_set3$cluster == i, ]
    
    # Exclude 'cluster' variable
    train_set_cluster$cluster <- NULL
    test_set_cluster$cluster <- NULL
    
    # Fit the model
    log_model <- glm(HeartDisease ~ ., family="binomial", data=train_set_cluster)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions <- predict(log_model, newdata=test_set_cluster, type="response")
    
    # Convert predictions to binary outcome
    predictions_bin <- ifelse(predictions > 0.5, 1, 0)
    
    # Compute accuracy
    accuracy <- sum(predictions_bin == test_set_cluster$HeartDisease) / nrow(test_set_cluster)
    
    # Add results to the data frame
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(Iteration = iter, Cluster = i, Model = "Logistic Regression", Accuracy = accuracy))
    
    # Fit the Random Forest model
    rf_model <- randomForest(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set_cluster, importance=TRUE)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions_rf <- predict(rf_model, newdata=test_set_cluster)
    
    # Compute accuracy
    accuracy_rf <- sum(predictions_rf == test_set_cluster$HeartDisease) / nrow(test_set_cluster)
    
    # Add results to the data frame
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(Iteration = iter, Cluster = i, Model = "Random Forest", Accuracy = accuracy_rf))
    
    # Fit the SVM model
    svm_model <- svm(HeartDisease ~ ., data=train_set_cluster, kernel = "radial", cost = 10, scale = FALSE)
    
    # Make predictions
    predictions_svm <- predict(svm_model, newdata=test_set_cluster)
    
    # Compute accuracy
    accuracy_svm <- sum(predictions_svm == test_set_cluster$HeartDisease) / nrow(test_set_cluster)
    
    # Add results to the data frame
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(Iteration = iter, Cluster = i, Model = "SVM", Accuracy = accuracy_svm))
  }
}

# Create a mapping for cluster names
cluster_names <- c(
  "1" = "Young-Healthy ",
  "2" = "Old-Medium Risk",
  "3" = "High-Risk"
)

# Create a color mapping for each model 
model_colors <- c(
  "Logistic Regression" = "darkred",
  "Random Forest" = "darkgreen",
  "SVM" = "gold"
)

# Calculate average accuracy and standard error for each model and cluster
avg_results <- results %>%
  group_by(Model, Cluster) %>%
  summarise(Avg_Accuracy = mean(Accuracy),
            SE_Accuracy = sd(Accuracy) / sqrt(n()),
            .groups='keep') %>%
  mutate(Cluster_Name = factor(cluster_names[as.character(Cluster)], levels = cluster_names))

# Plot the average accuracy with error bars
ggplot(avg_results, aes(x=Cluster_Name, y=Avg_Accuracy, fill=Model)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", position=position_dodge(), aes(fill = Model)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_colors) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=Avg_Accuracy-SE_Accuracy, ymax=Avg_Accuracy+SE_Accuracy), 
                position=position_dodge(0.9), width=0.2) +
  labs(x="Cluster", y="Average Accuracy", title="Average Model Performance by Cluster with Error Bars") +
  theme_minimal()


# Attempt to sort cluster by attributes

# Calculate the mean of each numeric attribute for each cluster
numeric_vars <- sapply(df, is.numeric)
mean_by_cluster <- aggregate(df[numeric_vars], by=list(df$cluster), mean)

# Calculate the mode of each categorical attribute for each cluster
categorical_vars <- sapply(df, is.factor)
mode_by_cluster <- lapply(df[categorical_vars], function(x) tapply(x, df$cluster, function(x) names(which.max(table(x)))))

# Combine both data frames into one for easy viewing
attributes_by_cluster <- cbind(mean_by_cluster, mode_by_cluster)


# Cluster summaries

# Summary statistics for Cluster 1
print("Summary Statistics for Cluster 1")
summary(df[df$cluster == 1,])

# Summary statistics for Cluster 2
print("Summary Statistics for Cluster 2")
summary(df[df$cluster == 2,])

# Summary statistics for Cluster 3
print("Summary Statistics for Cluster 3")
summary(df[df$cluster == 3,])


```


```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=TRUE,echo=TRUE}
# Compute and plot wss for k = 2 to k = 10
k.max <- 10
wss <- sapply(1:k.max, 
              function(k){kmeans(df_encoded, k, nstart=50, iter.max = 15 )$tot.withinss})

plot(1:k.max, wss,
     type="b", pch = 19, frame = FALSE, 
     xlab="Number of clusters K",
     ylab="Total within-clusters sum of squares")


```


## **PCA**

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}
## PCA

set.seed(123)

# Performing PCA
pca_result <- prcomp(scaled_data)

# Summarize the importance of components
summary(pca_result)

# Visualizing the data using the first two principal components
pca_data <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x)
pca_data$cluster <- df$cluster  # Add cluster information to the PCA data

# Plotting the first two principal components

ggplot(pca_data, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color=as.factor(cluster))) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(color="Cluster") +
  ggtitle("PCA Plot of First Two Principal Components")


# Scree Plot
plot(pca_result, type="l", main="Scree Plot")

# Biplot
biplot(pca_result, main="Biplot of First Two Principal Components")

##

# Fit the Random Forest model on the training data
rf_model_kmeans <- randomForest(cluster ~ ., data=train_set3, ntree=100)

# Make predictions on the test data
predictions_kmeans <- predict(rf_model_kmeans, newdata=test_set3)

# Evaluate the model
confusionMatrix(predictions_kmeans, test_set3$cluster)



###


# Repition for safety

d <- daisy(scaled_data, metric = "gower")
fit <- hclust(d)


k <- 3 
clusters_hierarchical <- cutree(fit, k)


df$cluster_hierarchical <- clusters_hierarchical

table(df$cluster)
# Summary statistics for hierarchical clusters
aggregate(.~ cluster_hierarchical, data=df, mean) # for new cluster summaries


# Visualizing the data using the first two principal components
pca_data <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x)
pca_data$clusterh <- df$cluster_hierarchical  # Add cluster information to the PCA data

# Plotting the first two principal components

ggplot(pca_data, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color=as.factor(clusterh))) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(color="Cluster") +
  ggtitle("PCA Plot of First Two Principal Components")


# 
trainIndex4 <- sample(1:nrow(df), 0.8 * nrow(df))
train_set4 <- df[trainIndex4, ]
test_set4 <- df[-trainIndex4, ]




# Converting cluster_hierarchical to factor again
train_set4$cluster_hierarchical <- as.factor(train_set4$cluster_hierarchical)
test_set4$cluster_hierarchical <- as.factor(test_set4$cluster_hierarchical)

# Build Random Forest Model
rf_model <- randomForest(cluster_hierarchical ~ ., data=train_set4, ntree=100)

# Predict on test data
predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata=test_set4)



confusionMatrix(predictions,test_set4$cluster_hierarchical)

```

## **Final model tuning**

```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}
## lasso


# For the logistic regression with stepwise selection
library(MASS)

full_model <- glm(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set2, family = binomial)
step_model <- stepAIC(full_model, direction = "backward")
summary(step_model)

# Make predictions on the validation set
step_model_preds_prob <- predict(step_model, newdata = test_set2, type = "response")
step_model_preds <- ifelse(step_model_preds_prob > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Compute performance metrics
step_model_accuracy <- sum(step_model_preds ==
                             test_set2$HeartDisease_1) / nrow(test_set2)
step_model_auc <- pROC::auc(pROC::roc(test_set2$HeartDisease_1, 
                                      step_model_preds_prob))


# For the logistic regression with lasso
library(glmnet)

x_train <- model.matrix(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = train_set2)[, -1]
y_train <- train_set2$HeartDisease_1

x_val <- model.matrix(HeartDisease_1 ~ ., data = test_set2)[, -1]
y_val <- test_set2$HeartDisease_1

lasso_model <- glmnet(x_train, y_train, family = "binomial", alpha = 1)
cv.lasso <- cv.glmnet(x_train, y_train, family = "binomial", alpha = 1)
best_lambda <- cv.lasso$lambda.min

# Make predictions on the validation set
lasso_model_preds_prob <- predict(lasso_model, newx = x_val, 
                                  s = best_lambda, type = "response")
lasso_model_preds <- ifelse(lasso_model_preds_prob > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Compute performance metrics
lasso_model_accuracy <- 
  sum(lasso_model_preds == 
        test_set2$HeartDisease_1) / nrow(test_set2)
lasso_model_auc <- pROC::auc(pROC::roc(test_set2$HeartDisease_1, 
                                       lasso_model_preds_prob))


# the metrics for comparison
cat("Stepwise selection logistic regression - Accuracy: ", 
    step_model_accuracy, " AUC: ", step_model_auc, "\n")
cat("Lasso logistic regression - Accuracy: ", lasso_model_accuracy, 
    " AUC: ", lasso_model_auc, "\n")

```


## **Shiny App**
```{r,warning=FALSE,message=FALSE,eval=FALSE,echo=TRUE}
# Load required libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(caret)
library(e1071)
library(randomForest)
library(clustMixType)
library(shiny)
library(shinythemes)
library(ggplot2)
library(cluster)



# Read and preprocess the data
df <- read.csv("heart2.csv")

# Pre-process
df$Sex <- as.factor(df$Sex)
df$ChestPainType <- as.factor(df$ChestPainType)
df$RestingECG <- as.factor(df$RestingECG)
df$ExerciseAngina <- as.factor(df$ExerciseAngina)
df$ST_Slope <- as.factor(df$ST_Slope)
df$FastingBS = as.factor(df$FastingBS)
df$HeartDisease = as.factor(df$HeartDisease)


# Load pre-computed results and trained models
rf_model <- readRDS("rf_model.rds")
results <- readRDS("results.rds")
train_set_cluster <- readRDS("train_set_cluster.rds")




# User Interface
ui <- fluidPage(
  theme = shinytheme("cerulean"),  # Apply the Cerulean theme
  titlePanel("Heart Disease Prediction"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      numericInput("age", "Age", 50, min = 1, max = 100),
      selectInput("sex", "Sex", choices = c("F", "M")),
      selectInput("chest_pain_type", "Chest Pain Type", choices = c("ASY", "ATA", "NAP", "TA")),
      numericInput("resting_bp", "Resting Blood Pressure", 120, min = 80, max = 200),
      numericInput("cholesterol", "Cholesterol", 200, min = 100, max = 500),
      selectInput("fasting_bs", "Fasting Blood Sugar > 120 mg/dl", choices = c("0", "1")),
      selectInput("resting_ecg", "Resting ECG", choices = c("LVH", "Normal", "ST")),
      numericInput("max_hr", "Max Heart Rate Achieved", 150, min = 60, max = 200),
      selectInput("exercise_angina", "Exercise Induced Angina", choices = c("N", "Y")),
      numericInput("oldpeak", "Oldpeak", 1, min = 0, max = 4),
      selectInput("st_slope", "ST Slope", choices = c("Down", "Flat", "Up"))
    ),
    
    mainPanel(
      HTML("<h3>Cluster Prediction</h3>"),
      textOutput("cluster_description"),
      HTML("<h3>Risk of Heart Failure</h3>"),
      textOutput("prediction"),
      plotOutput("densityPlot"),  
      plotOutput("ageRiskPlot")  
    )
  )
)

# Define server logic 
server <- function(input, output) {
  
  # Reactive values for patient's information
  new_patient <- reactive({
    data.frame(
      Age = as.numeric(input$age),
      Sex = factor(input$sex, levels = levels(train_set_cluster$Sex)),
      ChestPainType = factor(input$chest_pain_type, 
                             levels = levels(train_set_cluster$ChestPainType)),
      RestingBP = as.numeric(input$resting_bp),
      Cholesterol = as.numeric(input$cholesterol),
      FastingBS = factor(input$fasting_bs, 
                         levels = levels(train_set_cluster$FastingBS)),
      RestingECG = factor(input$resting_ecg, 
                          levels = levels(train_set_cluster$RestingECG)),
      MaxHR = as.numeric(input$max_hr),
      ExerciseAngina = factor(input$exercise_angina, 
                              levels = levels(train_set_cluster$ExerciseAngina)),
      Oldpeak = as.numeric(input$oldpeak),
      ST_Slope = factor(input$st_slope, 
                        levels = levels(train_set_cluster$ST_Slope))
    )
  })
  
  
  
  # Compute the risk for the entire dataset, so i can plot the density
  
  risk_all <- predict(rf_model, train_set_cluster, type = "prob")[, 2]
  
  # adding a reactive plot here
  output$densityPlot <- renderPlot({
    # use ggplot to create the density plot
    require(ggplot2)
    
    # Build the data frame for plotting
    plot_df <- data.frame(Risk = risk_all,
                          Cluster = factor(cluster_pred, 
                                           levels = unique(cluster_pred),
                                           labels = paste0("Cluster ", 
                                                           unique(cluster_pred))))
    
    # Plot density of Risk for each Cluster
    ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Risk, fill = Cluster)) +
      geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
      labs(x = "Predicted Risk of Heart Disease",
           y = "Density",
           title = "Density plot of Predicted Risk per Cluster") +
      theme_minimal()
  })
  
  # Train the clustering model on the training data
  gower_dist <- daisy(train_set_cluster[, -12], metric = "gower")
  clust_model <- hclust(gower_dist)
  cluster_pred <- cutree(clust_model, k = 3) 
  # store cluster predictions for the training set
  
  # Calculate the centroids of the existing clusters
  centroids <- aggregate(.~ cluster_pred, data = train_set_cluster[, -12], FUN = mean)
  
  # Predict the cluster for the new patient by finding the nearest centroid
  output$cluster <- renderText({
    distances <- apply(centroids[, -1], 1, function(x) sum((x - as.numeric(new_patient()))^2))
    new_patient_cluster <- which.min(distances)
    return(paste("The patient is predicted to belong to cluster", new_patient_cluster))
  })
  
  output$cluster_description <- renderText({
    distances <- apply(centroids[, -1], 1, function(x) sum((x - as.numeric(new_patient()))^2))
    new_patient_cluster <- which.min(distances)
    
    cluster_description <- switch(new_patient_cluster,
                                  "1" = "which represents patients who are 
                                  typically younger with mild symptoms of chest
                                  pain and normal resting ECG. These individuals
                                  often have controlled cholesterol levels and
                                  display no exercise-induced angina. 
                                  The predicted risk of heart disease is 
                                  generally lower for this group.",
                                  
                                  "2" = "which includes individuals who are 
                                  middle-aged with moderate symptoms such as 
                                  elevated blood pressure and occasional angina
                                  during exercise. They may have slightly higher
                                  cholesterol levels and present abnormalities 
                                  in resting ECG. The risk of heart disease is 
                                  moderate for this cluster.",
                                  
                                  "3" = "which is characterized by older 
                                  patients with severe symptoms like persistent 
                                  chest pain (e.g., ASY or ATA types), 
                                  high resting blood pressure, and significant
                                  exercise-induced angina. Cholesterol levels 
                                  are often elevated, and resting ECG might 
                                  show signs like LVH or ST changes. 
                                  The predicted risk of heart disease is higher
                                  for patients in this cluster."
    )
    
    return(paste("The patient is predicted to belong to cluster", new_patient_cluster, "\n", cluster_description))
  })
  
  
  # Predict the risk of heart disease
  output$prediction <- renderText({
    prob <- predict(rf_model, new_patient(), type = "prob")
    risk <- round(prob[2] * 100, 2)
    return(paste("The predicted risk of heart disease is", risk, "%"))
  })
  
  # 
  risk_all <- predict(rf_model, train_set_cluster, type = "prob")[, 2]
  
  # New plot for Feature Value vs Risk
  output$ageRiskPlot <- renderPlot({
    # Build data frame for plotting
    plot_df <- data.frame(Age = train_set_cluster$Age, 
                          Risk = risk_all)
    
    # Compute the risk for the new patient
    new_patient_risk <- predict(rf_model, new_patient(), type = "prob")[2]
    
    # Define error margin (initially 5% of risk) but later use model standard error
    error_margin <- new_patient_risk * 0.05
    
    # Plot the risk with age
    ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Age, y = Risk)) +
      geom_point(alpha=0.4) +  # plot points for all patients in training set
      geom_point(aes(x = input$age, y = new_patient_risk), colour = "red",
                 size = 3) +  # highlight the new patient in red
      geom_point(aes(x = input$age, y = new_patient_risk), shape = 1, 
                 size = error_margin * 500, stroke = 1, colour = "red",
                 alpha = 0.5) +  
      # Draw a circle around the new patient
      labs(x = "Age",
           y = "Predicted Risk of Heart Disease",
           title = "Age vs Predicted Risk of Heart Disease") +
      theme_bw() +
      theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))  # centering the plot title
  })
}


# Run the application 
shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)
```
